quality.rationale: 结合共形坐标变换与黎曼张量代数恒等式，经爱因斯坦场方程与理想流体质能动张量代换，逻辑链条完整且具明确宇宙学物理背景

origin.question:
Problem 19.8. Show by an explicit coordinate transformation that the Robertson-Walker metric is conformally flat. Write $\mathbf {R}_{\mu 
u a eta}$ in terms of $\mathbf {g}_{\mu 
u}$ and $ho, p$ and the 4-velocity $\mathfrak {u}^{\mu}$ of the matter.

## Solution
origin.answer: $R_{\\mu\\nu\\alpha\\beta} = 4\\pi \\left[ (\\rho+p)(u_\\mu u_\\beta g_{\\nu\\alpha} - u_\\mu u_\\alpha g_{\\nu\\beta} + u_\\nu u_\\alpha g_{\\mu\\beta} - u_\\nu u_\\beta g_{\\mu\\alpha}) - (\\rho-p)(g_{\\mu\\alpha}g_{\\nu\\beta} - g_{\\mu\\beta}g_{\\nu\\alpha}) \\right]$
origin.solution:
Solution 19.8. Start with the $\eta, \chi$ form of the metric
$$
\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \mathrm {R}^{2} (\eta) \left[ - \mathrm {d} \eta^{2} + \mathrm {d} \chi^{2} + \Sigma^{2} (\chi) \mathrm {d} \Omega^{2} \right] 
$$
(3rd form in Problem 19.5), where $\Sigma(\chi) = \sin h \chi$ , $\chi$ , $\sin \chi$ for $k = -1, 0, +1$ . Since conformal transformations preserve light cones, a natural first step is to go to null coordinates, e.g.
$$
\mathrm {u} = \frac {1} {2} (\eta + \chi) 
$$
$$
\mathrm {v} = \frac {1} {2} (\eta - \chi)
$$
The metric now becomes
$$
\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \mathrm {R}^{2} (\mathrm {u} + \mathrm {v}) [ - 4 \mathrm {d u d v} + \Sigma^{2} (\mathrm {u} - \mathrm {v}) \mathrm {d} \Omega^{2} ]. 
$$
We now try a transformation which preserves the “‘nullness’” of the coordinates, and also preserves the symmetry between u and v:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm {a} = \mathrm {g} (\mathrm {u}) \quad \mathrm {u} = \mathrm {f} (\alpha) \\
\beta = \mathbf {g} (\mathbf {v}) \quad \mathbf {v} = \mathbf {f} (\beta) \\ \end{array}
$$
where $g$ is the inverse function to $f$ . With this transformation, Equation (3) becomes
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \mathrm {R}^{2} (\mathrm {u} + \mathrm {v}) \left[ - 4 \mathrm {f}^{\prime} (\alpha) \mathrm {f}^{\prime} (\beta) \mathrm {d} \alpha \mathrm {d} \beta + \Sigma^{2} (\mathrm {u} - \mathrm {v}) \mathrm {d} \Omega^{2} \right] \\
= \mathrm {R}^{2} (\mathrm {u} + \mathrm {v}) \mathrm {f}^{\prime} (\alpha) \mathrm {f}^{\prime} (\beta) \left[ - 4 \mathrm {d} \alpha \mathrm {d} \beta + \frac {\sum^{2} (\mathrm {u} - \mathrm {v})}{\mathrm {f}^{\prime} (\alpha) \mathrm {f}^{\prime} (\beta)} \mathrm {d} \Omega^{2} \right]. \\
\end{array}
$$
If the metric is to be conformally flat, the term in brackets must have the flat space form $[-4\mathrm{d}\alpha \mathrm{d}\beta + (\alpha - \beta)^2\mathrm{d}\Omega^2]$ . Using $\mathbf{f}'(\alpha) =  rac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{u}}{\mathrm{d}\alpha} = \left( rac{\mathrm{d}\alpha}{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{u}}ight)^{-1} = [\mathbf{g}'(\mathbf{u})]^{-1}$ , we get the condition
$$
\mathbf {g}^{\prime} (\mathbf {u}) \mathbf {g}^{\prime} (\mathbf {v}) [ \boldsymbol {\Sigma} (\mathbf {u} - \mathbf {v}) ]^{2} = [ \mathbf {g} (\mathbf {u}) - \mathbf {g} (\mathbf {v}) ]^{2}. 
$$
For the case $k = 0$ , we have $\Sigma(u - v) = u - v$ , so $g(x) = x$ is a solution by inspection. For the other cases Equation (6) can be solved by first finding the solution for $v$ close to $u$ ; that is, for $v = u + \epsilon$ . Expanding Equation (6) in a Taylor series gives
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left(\mathrm {g}^{\prime}\right)^{2} \left[ 1 + \epsilon \mathrm {g}^{\prime \prime} / \mathrm {g}^{\prime} + \epsilon^{2} \mathrm {g}^{\prime \prime \prime} / (2 \mathrm {g}^{\prime}) \dot s \right] \epsilon^{2} \left(1 - k \epsilon^{2} / 6 \dot s\right)^{2} \\
= \epsilon^{2} (\mathrm {g}^{\prime})^{2} \left[ 1 + \epsilon \mathrm {g}^{\prime \prime} / (2 \mathrm {g}^{\prime}) + \epsilon^{2} \mathrm {g}^{\prime \prime \prime} / (6 \mathrm {g}^{\prime}) \dot s \right]^{2}, \\
\end{array}
$$
so that with $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{g}'$ , $\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{p}'$ and $\mathbf{k} = +1$ , we must solve
$$
2 q d q / d p - 4 p = 3 q^{2} / p.
$$
This Bernoulli differential equation gives
$$
q = p (A p - 4 )^{\frac {1} {2}}, \quad A = c o n s t a n t,
$$
and integrating twice we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
u + B = \tan^{- 1} (A p / 4 - 1 )^{\frac {1} {2}} \\
\mathbf {g} (\mathfrak {u}) = \mathbf {C} \tan (\mathfrak {u} + \mathbf {B}) + \mathbf {D}. \\
\end{array}
$$
We can now verify by substitution that this is in fact the general solution to Equation (6). Without loss of generality we can take $\mathbf{g} = 	an u$ . For $k = -1$ , we can similarly find $\mathbf{g} = 	an h u$ . With these solutions, Equation (6) turns into the trigonometric identities
$$
\sec u \sec v \sin (u - v) = \tan u - \tan v
$$
sech u sech v sinh (u-v) = tanh u - tanh v
and the metric of Equation (5) becomes
$$
\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \frac {\mathrm {R}^{2} \left[ \tan^{- 1} (\alpha) + \tan^{- 1} (\beta) \right]}{(1 + \alpha^{2}) \left(1 + \beta^{2}\right)} \{- 4 \mathrm {d} \alpha \mathrm {d} \beta + (\alpha - \beta )^{2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^{2} \}
or
$$
$$
\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \frac {\mathrm {R}^{2} \left[ \tan h^{- 1} (\alpha) + \tan h^{- 1} (\beta) \right]}{(1 - \alpha^{2}) \left(1 - \beta^{2}\right)} \{- 4 \mathrm {d} \alpha \mathrm {d} \beta + (\alpha - \beta )^{2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^{2} \}
$$
which is manifestly conformally flat. A conformally flat metric has zero Weyl tensor, so the Riemann tensor is only composed of the Ricci tensor and scalar curvature (see the introduction to Chapter 9).
$$
\mathsf {R}_{\gamma \delta}^{\alpha \beta} = 2 \delta_{[ \gamma}^{[ \alpha} \mathsf {R}_{\delta ]}^{\beta ]} - \frac {1} {3} \delta_{[ \gamma}^{[ \alpha} \delta_{\delta ]}^{\beta ]} \mathsf {R}. 
$$
With this the Einstein equation
$$
\mathrm {R}_{\beta}^{\alpha} = 8 \pi \left(\mathrm {T}_{\beta}^{\alpha} - \frac {1} {2} \mathrm {g}_{\beta}^{\alpha} \mathrm {T}\right)
$$
and the stress energy tensor
$$
\mathbf {T}_{\beta}^{\alpha} = (\rho + \mathrm {p}) \mathbf {u}^{\alpha} \mathbf {u}_{\beta} + \mathrm {p g}_{\beta}^{\alpha}
$$
give $\mathbf{R}^{\alpha  eta}_{\gamma \delta}$ in terms of $\mathbf{g}_{\mu 
u},\mathfrak{u}^{\mu},ho ,\mathfrak{p}$
